1.pericope_label: Peter and John Before the Council
1.synthesis: While Peter is still speaking, the priests, the captain of the temple, and the Sadducees seize the apostles, "greatly annoyed because they were teaching the people and proclaiming in Jesus the resurrection from the dead" (vv.1–3); yet many believe, and the number of men grows to about five thousand (v.4). Brought before the rulers, elders, and scribes and asked "By what power or by what name did you do this?" (vv.5–7), Peter, filled with the Holy Spirit, answers that the lame man stands healed "by the name of Jesus Christ of Nazareth, whom you crucified, whom God raised" — the rejected stone now become the cornerstone (vv.8–11). He concludes: "There is salvation in no one else" (v.12).
1.historical_context: The "captain of the temple" (v.1) was a senior priestly officer second only to the high priest. The Sadducees, the priestly aristocracy who accepted only the Pentateuch and denied bodily resurrection (cf. Acts 23:8), are "greatly annoyed" precisely because the apostles proclaim "in Jesus the resurrection" (v.2) — both an empirical claim that one man rose and a theological provocation. Peter's Spirit-filling for his defense (v.8) fulfills Jesus' promise that the Spirit would teach his disciples what to say before authorities (Luke 12:11–12). The "cornerstone" of Psalms 118:22 (v.11) was already a recognized messianic text, which Jesus himself had turned against this same council (Luke 20:17).
1.literary_structure: This opens the first of the church's trial scenes, the pattern of arrest, interrogation, and bold testimony that will recur through Acts and fulfill Jesus' predictions of persecution (Luke 21:12–15). The leaders' question, "By what name?" (v.7), hands Peter his theme: the name of Jesus, which dominates the defense and its climax (v.10, v.12). Peter maps Psalms 118:22 onto the legal moment: the council are "the builders" who rejected, God is the architect who vindicated. Luke's notice of growth amid opposition (v.4) sounds his refrain that persecution cannot stop the word.
1.theological_application: The Spirit turns an interrogation into a pulpit: Peter, hauled before the highest court, preaches Christ rather than pleads for himself (vv.8–10), the promised help in the hour of trial. The exclusive claim of v.12 — "there is no other name under heaven… by which we must be saved" — remains the church's bedrock confession, culturally scandalous then and now, that salvation is found in Christ alone. And the growth of the church under pressure (v.4) assures believers that opposition is no sign of God's absence.
1.christology: Peter sets two verdicts side by side: "whom you crucified, whom God raised" (v.10) — the council's rejection answered by God's vindication, exactly the rejected-stone-become-cornerstone of Psalms 118:22 (v.11). The cornerstone is the stone that aligns the whole building; Christ is thus the foundation and norm of God's new people. The absolute claim that salvation is in no other name (v.12) confesses Jesus as the sole and sufficient Savior, the divinely appointed (dei) means by which anyone is rescued.

13.pericope_label: The Boldness of Peter and John
13.synthesis: The council marvels at the "boldness of Peter and John," perceiving that they were "uneducated, common men," and they recognize that they had been with Jesus (v.13). With the healed man standing beside them, they can say nothing against it (v.14). Unable to deny "a notable sign," they confer and resort to threats, charging the apostles to speak no more in Jesus' name (vv.15–18). Peter and John answer with the principle that will echo through Acts: "Whether it is right in the sight of God to listen to you rather than to God, you must judge, for we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard" (vv.19–20). Further threatened, they are released, and the people glorify God for the miracle (vv.21–22).
13.historical_context: That the apostles are "uneducated, common men" (agrammatoi kai idiōtai, v.13) means not illiterate but untrained in formal scribal and legal learning — Galilean laymen, not Jerusalem scholars, which makes their bold reasoning astonishing to the court. The recognition that "they had been with Jesus" (v.13) traces their boldness to their Master. Peter's reply — that it is right to obey God rather than men (v.19) — follows the ancient pattern of appeal to a higher authority than the tribunal, like Socrates' "I shall obey the god rather than you," and grounds the church's later civil disobedience. The man healed was "more than forty years old" (v.22), underscoring the undeniable scale of the sign.
13.literary_structure: The scene turns on the contrast between the council's helplessness and the apostles' freedom: the leaders "could say nothing in opposition" (v.14) and fall back on threats (v.17), while Peter and John speak with parrēsia, the bold candor that is a key Lukan virtue (v.13; cf. Acts 4:29, Acts 4:31). The principle "we cannot but speak" (v.20) crystallizes the theme of irrepressible witness and pairs with its near-twin in Acts 5:29. The presence of the healed man as living, unanswerable evidence (v.14) ties this scene firmly to the sign that began it.
13.theological_application: The world takes note when ordinary people have "been with Jesus" (v.13): boldness and wisdom beyond one's training are the fruit of communion with Christ, not credentials. The apostles establish the limit of every human authority — when a command of men contradicts the command of God, "we must obey God" (v.19) — a charter for faithful conscience under pressure. And the compulsion "we cannot but speak of what we have seen and heard" (v.20) names the inner necessity of genuine witness: those who have truly met the risen Christ cannot keep silent.
13.christology: The boldness of the apostles points back to its source — they "had been with Jesus" (v.13), and his risen life now overflows in them. The undeniable sign (v.16) testifies that Jesus continues to act, so that the council's prohibition is, in effect, an attempt to silence the living Lord. The apostles' refusal to stop speaking "in this name" (v.18) confesses that allegiance to Christ outranks every earthly court, for he is the one in whose name alone salvation is found (Acts 4:12).

23.synthesis: Released, Peter and John return to their own and report the threats, and the whole company lifts its voice together to God in prayer (vv.23–24). They address the Sovereign Lord who made all things, and read the opposition of "Herod and Pontius Pilate, with the Gentiles and the peoples of Israel" through Psalms 2: the rulers raged, yet only did "whatever your hand and your plan had predestined" (vv.25–28). They do not pray for safety but for boldness: "grant to your servants to continue to speak your word with all boldness" while God stretches out his hand to heal (vv.29–30). The place is shaken, and they are all filled afresh with the Holy Spirit and speak the word with boldness (v.31).
23.historical_context: The prayer opens by addressing God as "Sovereign Lord" (Despota, v.24), the title of absolute mastery, and quotes the creation language of Exodus 20:11 and Psalms 146:6 — the Maker of all can act in all. Its center is Psalms 2:1–2 (vv.25–26), whose four categories (kings, rulers, nations, peoples) map exactly onto the crucifixion's agents — Herod, Pilate, Gentiles, and Israel (v.27; cf. the Herod-Pilate scene in Luke 23:6–12). The request for parrēsia ("boldness," v.29) in the face of a gag order is a prayer for courage to keep obeying God, and the shaking of the place (v.31) is an Old Testament theophany sign (cf. Exodus 19:18; Isaiah 6:4).
23.literary_structure: The episode completes a recurring Lukan rhythm: apostolic action, opposition, return to the community, corporate prayer, and divine confirmation (v.31). The prayer is a model of scripture-shaped petition, praying "in the words" of Psalms 2 and reading the present crisis through the Psalm's lens of raging rulers and God's enthroned purpose. The shaking and renewed filling with the Spirit (v.31) deliberately echo Pentecost (Acts 2:4), presenting this as a fresh empowering of the same community, and answer the prayer's exact request — boldness to speak the word.
23.theological_application: The church's instinct under threat is to gather and pray together (v.24), and its prayer is striking: not for the removal of danger but for boldness within it (v.29). This reorders Christian petition toward faithfulness over comfort. The believers also find courage by setting the crisis within God's sovereignty — even Herod and Pilate did only what God's hand had "predestined to take place" (v.28) — so that confidence in God's rule, not denial of the threat, frees them to obey. The God who controls all creation can be trusted to act in their particular trial.
23.christology: The prayer reads the cross through Psalms 2, identifying Jesus as God's "holy servant" and "Anointed" (v.27) against whom the rulers raged in vain. The staggering claim of v.28 — that the conspirators accomplished exactly what God had foreordained — holds together human guilt and divine sovereignty in the death of Christ, the same pairing as Acts 2:23. The continued signs done "through the name of your holy servant Jesus" (v.30) confess that the crucified and risen Messiah is still at work, his name the channel of God's healing power.

32.synthesis: Luke gives a second portrait of the community: "the full number of those who believed were of one heart and soul," holding nothing as their own but sharing everything (v.32). With great power the apostles bear witness to the resurrection, and great grace is upon them all (v.33). "There was not a needy person among them," for those who owned lands or houses sold them and laid the proceeds at the apostles' feet, distributed as any had need (vv.34–35). Luke names a model: Joseph, called Barnabas, "son of encouragement," a Levite from Cyprus, who sold a field and brought the money (vv.36–37).
32.historical_context: "Of one heart and soul" (v.32) echoes both the classical ideal of perfect friendship and the command to love God with "all your heart and soul" (Deuteronomy 6:5). "There was not a needy person among them" (v.34) deliberately fulfills the covenant ideal "there will be no poor among you" (Deuteronomy 15:4). This sharing was voluntary and occasional, not compelled communism — Peter will affirm to Ananias that property remained his to keep (Acts 5:4) — distinguishing it from the regulated communal ownership of the Essenes. Barnabas (v.36), a Levite from Cyprus, becomes the bridge figure who will later vouch for Paul (Acts 9:27) and partner him in mission.
32.literary_structure: This is the second of Luke's idealized community summaries (cf. Acts 2:42–47; Acts 5:12–16), framing the narrative with a picture of the church's character — here, its unity and generosity. The introduction of Barnabas as a positive example (vv.36–37) is set up deliberately as a foil to the negative example of Ananias and Sapphira that immediately follows (Acts 5:1) — Luke's exempla technique of pairing a model with a counter-model. The central note that "great grace was upon them all" (v.33) ties the community's generosity to the power of the resurrection witness.
32.theological_application: Genuine faith reshapes the purse: those "of one heart and soul" no longer clutch possessions as their own but hold them open to a brother's need (v.32). The result is a community where "there was not a needy person" (v.34) — a living parable of the gospel's power to dissolve self-interest into shared life. Barnabas shows that such generosity is concrete and personal, not abstract (v.37), and the placement "at the apostles' feet" models stewardship under accountable leadership. The passage challenges every church to let resurrection power overflow into open-handed care.
32.christology: The community's life is grounded in the apostles' testimony to "the resurrection of the Lord Jesus" (v.33): their generosity is the social fruit of his risen lordship and the "great grace" that rests upon them. The fulfillment of the covenant ideal that there be no poor (v.34; cf. Deuteronomy 15:4) shows the messianic community embodying what Israel was always meant to be, now made possible by the reign of the exalted Christ. The shared life is, at bottom, the visible outworking of the Lord who gave himself for his people.
